summary: Jeff Atwood explores why people post cruel comments online, using examples of harassment directed at grieving parents. He argues that the abstraction of typing rather than face-to-face interaction enables cruelty, and that online abuse disproportionately targets women and minorities. Through examining cases of people who engaged with their harassers rather than simply blocking them, Atwood builds to his central thesis: people become cruel online because they're terrified. When confronted with evidence that tragedy can strike anyone randomly, the fear becomes unbearable, so they must make victims into monsters who somehow deserved their fate.
key_points: The abstraction of typing rather than speaking face-to-face enables people to say things online they would never say in person
Online abuse disproportionately targets women, people of color, and LGBTQ individuals, with straight white men largely immune from the worst harassment
Some remarkable individuals choose to engage harassers with empathy rather than simply blocking them, demonstrating the empathy their attackers lack
People post cruel things because facing the reality that tragedy can strike anyone randomly creates unbearable existential dread
By making victims into monsters who deserved their fate, people protect themselves from the terrifying truth that they have no control over random tragedy
money_quotes: As an exercise in empathy, try to imagine saying some of the terrible things people typed to each other online to a real person sitting directly in front of you.

It's easy to practice empathy when you limit it to people that are easy to empathize with – the downtrodden, the undeserving victims. But it is another matter entirely to empathize with those that hate, harangue, and intentionally make other people's lives miserable.

We want to believe that the world is understandable and controllable and unthreatening, that if we follow the rules, we'll be okay. So, when this kind of thing happens to other people, we need to put them in a different category from us.

When faced with the abyss of pain and grief that parents feel over the loss of their children, due to utter random chance in a world they can't control, the overwhelming sense of existential dread is simply too much to bear.
The weight of seeing through the fear and beyond the monster to simply discover yourself is often too terrible for many people to bear. In a world of heavy things, it's the heaviest there is.
key_insight: Online cruelty stems not from malice but from existential terror—when people see evidence that random tragedy can strike anyone, they must transform victims into monsters to preserve the illusion that following the rules keeps you safe.
